subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank/IDB, Bank of Jamaica, Freedom House, RSF, InSight Crime) + international and Jamaican press.

The Caribbean's standout fiscal-discipline success (debt cut from ~150% to ~65% of GDP, a credible inflation-targeting central bank) - but Hurricane Melissa (Cat 5, Oct 2025) was the costliest disaster in its history (~US$8.8bn, ~41% of 2024 GDP).
Standout features: institutional/fiscal strength and a homicide rate roughly halved in 2025, against a structural, recurring catastrophic-hurricane tail and still-high (if falling) violent crime.

economic.notes: A fiscal-discipline graduate with a credible central bank, Melissa a near-term pause not a reversal.

fiscal_state.notes: Debt fell toward ~65% of GDP (lowest in 25y), targeting the 60% FRL ceiling by FY27/28; Melissa reconstruction (US$6.7bn financing secured) pauses but does not reverse the trajectory. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The BOJ is a credible, independent inflation-targeter (4-6% band) - a genuine regional standout. (Bank of Jamaica)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The JMD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Converged into the 4-6% band in 2025, but Melissa is projected to push inflation to ~11.5% in mid-2026 before re-converging. (BOJ QMPR)

banking_stability.notes: Resilient in 2025 stress tests - low NPLs, strong capital/liquidity, reserves at a record ~US$6.1bn. (BOJ Financial Stability Report)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Tourism, remittances, and bauxite/alumina drive a current-account surplus; Melissa hit ~40-50% of hotels, denting near-term tourism. (IMF; World Bank/IDB)

institutional.notes: Functioning courts and credible data, with persistent corruption and a slipping (still strong) press.

rule_of_law.notes: Functioning courts and peaceful transfers, but corruption is persistent and official-organized-crime ties are flagged. (Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: STATIN, BOJ, and PIOJ produce credible, IMF-cited data with transparent post-disaster assessments. (IMF; World Bank)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Competent macro-fiscal and central-bank administration; the disaster-management apparatus is functional though stretched by Melissa. (IMF; UN)

press_freedom.notes: Among the freest in the Americas but sliding - RSF fell to ~26th (2025) from 6th in 2020. (RSF 2025; PAJ)

political_social.notes: A stable two-party democracy with falling homicides, against deep garrison-community divides.

political_stability.notes: The JLP (Holness) won a historic third consecutive term in Sept 2025 - peaceful, with no significant violence (low turnout ~39.5%). (ECJ; JIS)

civil_liberties.notes: Rated 'Free', though states of emergency raise civil-liberties/detention concerns. (Freedom House 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: No major xenophobia issue; standard-to-friendly treatment of tourists/expats. (observed)

social_cohesion.notes: Deep class/garrison-community divides and gang structures underpin violence, but a strong national identity and falling homicides point to gradual gains. (InSight Crime)

demographics.notes: Record-low unemployment (~3.7%) but persistent skilled-emigration/brain drain and ageing weigh long-term. (IMF)

geopolitical.notes: A stable, US/UK-aligned, conflict-free, unsanctioned democracy.

alliance_reliability.notes: A stable US/UK-aligned democracy and active CARICOM member with no adversarial alignments. (Freedom House)

conflict_involvement.notes: No external conflict; not party to the regional Venezuela disputes. (observed)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: An open economy with no sanctions or capital controls and a floating JMD. (IMF AREAER)

physical_practical.notes: The dominant national risk - extreme and rising hurricane exposure, with high crime.

climate.notes: 2025 Hurricane Melissa (Cat 5, ~41% of GDP) is the anchor - high SIDS exposure plus sea-level rise. (IPCC AR6)

healthcare.notes: A universal public system, functional but under-resourced and badly damaged by Melissa, with a private tier. (UN; World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: Decent pre-storm but Melissa devastated energy/transport/telecoms/schools; a US$6.7bn reconstruction is underway. (World Bank/IDB)

crime_safety.notes: Homicide ~23.7/100k in 2025 (halved from ~40, the first sub-700-murder year in 31 years) but still among the region's highest. (InSight Crime; JCF)

disaster_insurance.notes: Melissa insured losses possibly >US$4bn; a disaster-risk-financing pioneer (CCRIF, cat bond) but with a large protection gap. (IDB)

summary: The Caribbean's standout institutional and fiscal success - public debt slashed from ~150% to ~65% of GDP, a credible independent inflation-targeting central bank, record reserves, a resilient banking system, a stable two-party democracy (the JLP's historic third term, Sept 2025), and a homicide rate that roughly halved in 2025. The decisive, recurring drag is physical catastrophe risk: Hurricane Melissa (Cat 5, Oct 2025) inflicted ~US$8.8bn in damage (~41% of GDP), the costliest disaster in the nation's history, a year after Beryl. The country's hard-won macro credibility is precisely what lets it absorb such shocks (rapid US$6.7bn reconstruction financing, mature disaster-risk instruments), but the climate tail is structural and sets a negative overall skew.
